spec_match.rationale: Page demonstrates the SHAPE of the new crash report body but cannot trigger an actual unresponsiveness crash (that requires the Chrome unresponsive-page detector to fire, which kills the tab) — the demo simulates the payload instead of producing one.

interactive.rationale: Buttons to mint infinite-loop / deep-recursion / GC-pressure synthesised reports, with the resulting report body rendered in-page.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index blurb describes the headline and the page leads with it.

edge_cases.rationale: Missing: cross-origin frame inheriting the report, what happens when the call stack is too deep to serialise, what the report looks like when there is no JS on stack (idle freeze).

references.rationale: The see-also chromestatus link points at 5045134925660160 instead of 4731248572628992 — breaks the slug self-heal recovery.

openQuestions.title: Fix the chromestatus reference link
openQuestions.detail: Swap https://chromestatus.com/feature/5045134925660160 for https://chromestatus.com/feature/4731248572628992 so the slug self-heal script can recover this folder.

openQuestions.title: Add a real-but-survivable freeze button
openQuestions.detail: Currently all reports are synthesised. Add a button that runs a 10s blocking loop on a different frame (e.g. an iframe with allow="") so the user can see Chrome's real unresponsive detection fire and a real ReportingObserver event arrive — labelled as `may hang your tab`.

openQuestions.title: Show the idle-freeze case
openQuestions.detail: When the main thread is frozen by something that is NOT JS (e.g. a synchronous XHR off-thread), the call stack is empty. Synthesise that report shape so authors know what to expect.

summary: Synthesised demo of the report shape is useful but the broken chromestatus link is a blocker; adding a real freeze trigger would close the gap between simulation and spec behaviour.
